skill.name: ai-research-reproduction
skill.description: Guides README-first reproduction of AI research repositories. It selects a small documented target, records evidence, limits protocol changes, and writes standardized reproduction outputs.
skill.summary: Run careful, evidence-based reproduction workflows for AI research repositories.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits, but most markdown backtick, weak-crypto, path traversal, reconnaissance, and obfuscation findings are false positives after contextual review. The confirmed risk is that the helper can execute README-derived commands from an untrusted target repository when explicitly enabled, which is legitimate for this skill but requires a marketplace warning and user review.

medium_findings.title: README-Derived Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The orchestration script can execute a command extracted from the target repository README when --run-selected is enabled. This is expected for a reproduction skill, but a malicious or untrusted repository README could cause arbitrary local command execution in the target repo context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run executes shlex-split README-derived command strings with cwd set to the target repository. The behavior is gated by --run-selected and fits the skill purpose, so the risk is real but contextual.

medium_findings.title: Training Command Passed To External Runner
medium_findings.description: For selected training targets, the script forwards the README-derived training command to a separate run-train script. The reviewed file does not show the runner implementation, so the main concern is delegated execution of untrusted repository commands under the reproduction workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is clearly passed to another script with repo path, timeout, and training mode. The actual execution details are outside this file, so the confidence is high for delegated execution but lower for exact impact.

low_findings.title: Parent Directory Reference Loading
low_findings.description: The skill asks the agent to load reference files through ../../ paths. This appears to be shared documentation loading, not data exfiltration, but it expands the review boundary beyond the skill directory and should be checked during packaging.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced paths are explicit read-only documentation paths. They are not direct traversal exploits, but they can cause the skill to depend on files outside its package.

low_findings.title: Temporary Context File Creation
low_findings.description: The script writes a temporary JSON context file before invoking the bundle writer and removes it in a finally block. This is a normal implementation pattern with limited residual risk if process termination prevents cleanup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file contains generated context and is deleted in a finally block. No evidence shows secret collection or intentional persistence.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Markdown And Weak-Crypto False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static hits are markdown code fences, template placeholders, filenames, or ordinary prose such as Rigor Reproduce. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms, Ruby backtick execution, JS obfuscation, or prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain documentation, YAML prompt text, code fences, placeholders, and a shebang. Targeted review found no crypto implementation, Ruby execution, obfuscation payload, or injection text.

dangerous_patterns.title: README-Derived Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The orchestration script can execute a command extracted from the target repository README when --run-selected is enabled. This is expected for a reproduction skill, but a malicious or untrusted repository README could cause arbitrary local command execution in the target repo context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run executes shlex-split README-derived command strings with cwd set to the target repository. The behavior is gated by --run-selected and fits the skill purpose, so the risk is real but contextual.

dangerous_patterns.title: Training Command Passed To External Runner
dangerous_patterns.description: For selected training targets, the script forwards the README-derived training command to a separate run-train script. The reviewed file does not show the runner implementation, so the main concern is delegated execution of untrusted repository commands under the reproduction workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is clearly passed to another script with repo path, timeout, and training mode. The actual execution details are outside this file, so the confidence is high for delegated execution but lower for exact impact.

content.value_statement: AI research repositories are often hard to reproduce because setup, assets, commands, and claims are scattered. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a README-first workflow with evidence logs and conservative patch rules.
actual_capabilities: Reads repository documentation and nearby project signals before selecting a reproduction target.
Chooses the smallest documented inference, evaluation, or training startup target that can be verified.
Plans setup, asset, checkpoint, and dataset assumptions with explicit evidence labels.
Can run selected documented commands when the user enables execution.
Writes standardized reproduction outputs under repro_outputs and optional train_outputs.
Records assumptions, blockers, protocol deviations, and human review points.
limitations: It does not prove full paper reproduction from a smoke test or startup run.
It depends on repository documentation quality and available assets.
Command execution can run untrusted repository code when enabled.
It cannot resolve missing datasets, private checkpoints, or unavailable hardware by itself.

use_cases.title: Verify A New Research Repository
use_cases.description: Identify the smallest documented run, prepare setup assumptions, and record whether the repository can execute cleanly.

use_cases.title: Audit Reproduction Evidence
use_cases.description: Produce a concise evidence bundle that separates verified facts, assumptions, blockers, and protocol deviations.

use_cases.title: Prepare A Handoff Report
use_cases.description: Create reproducible notes, command records, and next safe actions for another engineer to continue the work.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Basic Reproduction
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin with a repository and no special constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-research-reproduction on this repository. Read the README first, choose the smallest documented target, and write repro_outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Run Without Patching
prompt_templates.scenario: The user wants evidence before any edits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-research-reproduction, but do not modify repository files. Record setup assumptions, blockers, and the safest documented command to try.

prompt_templates.title: Check Training Startup
prompt_templates.scenario: Only a training command is available.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-research-reproduction for a trusted training startup check. Do not claim full reproduction unless I explicitly authorize a longer run.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Failed Reproduction
prompt_templates.scenario: A prior run failed or produced incomplete results.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-research-reproduction to inspect the existing outputs, identify protocol deviations, classify blockers, and propose the next safe action.

output_examples.input: Reproduce this vision model repository from its README.
output: Selected documented inference as the minimum trustworthy target.
Recorded setup commands, asset assumptions, attempted command, blocker, and next safe action.
Generated SUMMARY.md, COMMANDS.md, LOG.md, status.json, and comparability notes.

output_examples.input: The repository only documents training. Verify it without overclaiming.
output: Selected training startup verification rather than full training.
Recorded checkpoint and dataset assumptions with human review points.
Marked fuller training as requiring explicit approval.

output_examples.input: Review the reproduction run and tell me what changed.
output: Summarized whether patches were applied and why.
Separated README-fidelity impact from ordinary setup notes.
Listed verified evidence and unresolved uncertainty.

best_practices: Run on trusted or isolated repositories because documented commands may execute local code.
Start with inference or evaluation before training when the README provides smaller targets.
Keep every setup change, patch, assumption, and deviation visible in the generated outputs.
anti_patterns: Do not treat a smoke test as a full paper reproduction result.
Do not silently change datasets, checkpoints, metrics, losses, or model semantics.
Do not run unreviewed README commands from untrusted repositories on a sensitive machine.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically reproduce any AI paper?
faq.answer: No. It creates a careful repository-grounded workflow and reports evidence, blockers, and limits.

faq.question: Can it run repository commands?
faq.answer: Yes, when execution is enabled. Users should review commands and use isolated environments for untrusted repositories.

faq.question: What outputs does it create?
faq.answer: It targets repro_outputs with summaries, commands, logs, status data, comparability notes, and patch records when needed.

faq.question: Does it modify source code?
faq.answer: It prefers no edits. If edits are required, they must be conservative, auditable, and documented.

faq.question: How does it handle long training runs?
faq.answer: It favors startup verification first and requires explicit user approval before fuller trusted training claims.

faq.question: Can it replace missing datasets or checkpoints?
faq.answer: No. It can record missing assets and suggest next steps, but it should not silently substitute different scientific inputs.
